k8s_privileged.description: Container running in privileged mode — grants full host kernel access, equivalent to root on the node. Enables container escape.
Pod shares host network namespace — container can bind to any host port and sniff host network traffic.
Pod shares host PID namespace — container can see and signal all host processes, enabling ptrace-based attacks on other containers.
Pod shares host IPC namespace — container can access host shared memory segments, enabling data leakage from other processes.
hostPath mount of /etc — container can read or overwrite host configuration files including /etc/passwd, /etc/sudoers, and cron configs.
Docker socket mounted into container — full container escape and arbitrary container creation on the host with a single API call.
hostPath mount of root filesystem (/) — container has read/write access to the entire host filesystem.
allowPrivilegeEscalation: true — container processes can acquire more privileges than their parent via setuid binaries or file capabilities.
Container explicitly runs as UID 0 (root) — any container escape grants root access to the underlying node.
runAsNonRoot: false — Kubernetes will not enforce that the container runs as a non-root user, even if the image supports it.

Dangerous Linux capability added to container. NET_ADMIN allows network reconfiguration; SYS_ADMIN is near-equivalent to privileged; SYS_PTRACE enables ptrace-based container escape; SYS_MODULE allows kernel module loading.
hostPath mount of /proc — grants access to host process information and kernel configuration, enabling container escape via /proc/sysrq-trigger or similar.
Root filesystem is writable — an attacker who compromises the container can modify binaries, install backdoors, or tamper with configuration files. Best practice is readOnlyRootFilesystem: true.

k8s_rbac.description: RBAC rule grants all verbs (*) — subject can perform get, list, watch, create, update, patch, delete on all matched resources.
RBAC rule targets all resources (*) — combined with broad verbs this grants cluster-wide data access including Secrets.
Binding to cluster-admin role — grants unrestricted access to all resources in all namespaces. Equivalent to full root access to the cluster.
Binding to system:masters group — this group bypasses all RBAC authorization checks and grants unconditional full cluster access. Cannot be revoked via RBAC policies.
RBAC rule targets all API groups (*) — includes core, apps, networking, and custom resource groups. Combined with wildcard resources and verbs = full cluster admin.
ClusterRoleBinding detected — grants permissions cluster-wide across all namespaces. Review the referenced role for overly broad permissions.
RBAC rule grants access to Secrets resource — service accounts with secret read access can exfiltrate all credentials stored in the namespace.

k8s_network.description: NetworkPolicy found — verify it is restrictive and not set to allow-all. Flag: absence of NetworkPolicy in a namespace means all pod-to-pod traffic is permitted.
NetworkPolicy has an empty ingress rule ({}) — allows all inbound traffic from any pod or external source. Effectively disables network isolation.
NetworkPolicy has an empty egress rule ({}) — allows all outbound traffic. Compromised pods can reach any internal or external service without restriction.
hostPort binding — exposes the container port directly on the node's IP, bypassing any load balancer or service mesh, and reduces scheduling flexibility.
Service of type LoadBalancer — provisions an internet-facing load balancer. Verify that only intended services are internet-exposed (not databases, admin UIs, or internal APIs).
Service of type NodePort — exposes a port on every cluster node. If nodes are internet-accessible, this bypasses ingress and firewall rules.

github_actions.description: GitHub Action referenced by mutable version tag (e.g., @v1, @v2.3) — the tag can be moved to point to malicious code. Pin to a full commit SHA for supply chain safety.
GitHub Action pinned to a branch name (main/master/latest/develop) — any push to that branch immediately affects all workflows using it. Extremely high supply chain risk.

GitHub Actions expression interpolating untrusted event data (issue/PR title, body, comment) directly into a run: step. Attacker-controlled text is executed as shell code. Set as environment variable first instead.
Workflow uses pull_request_target trigger — runs with write permissions in the context of the base branch. If combined with checkout of the PR head, untrusted code gains access to secrets.
Secret value echoed to stdout in run step — GitHub masks known secret values in logs, but concatenated or transformed secrets may not be masked and will appear in plaintext.
Insecure workflow commands enabled (ACTIONS_ALLOW_UNSECURE_COMMANDS=true) — allows set-env and add-path commands which can be injected via log output to override environment variables and PATH.
Workflow token has write-all permissions — grants write access to contents, pull-requests, packages, deployments, and secrets. Apply minimum required permissions per job.
workflow_dispatch input interpolated into run step — user-supplied input string executed as shell code. Assign to an environment variable and reference via $VAR_NAME instead.

curl piped to shell in GitHub Actions workflow — downloads and executes arbitrary remote code. Verify the URL is pinned/trusted and consider downloading, verifying integrity (SHA-256), then executing.
Workflow requests OIDC token write permission — used for cloud provider federation (AWS/GCP/Azure). Verify this is intentional and the workflow is not triggered by untrusted external events.

gitlab_ci.description: CI artifact with no expiry — artifacts may contain build outputs, test reports, or generated secrets that persist indefinitely and are accessible to all project members.
curl output piped directly to shell in GitLab CI script — downloads and executes arbitrary remote code without integrity verification.
GitLab CI Docker executor running in privileged mode — container has full access to the host Docker daemon, enabling container escape and access to all other containers on the runner.
Hardcoded credential or API key pattern found in GitLab CI variables block — visible to all users with repository access and stored in pipeline logs.
CI artifacts include sensitive file patterns (.env, production config, private keys, PEM files) — uploaded to GitLab artifact storage and downloadable by project members.

GIT_STRATEGY: clone performs a full fresh clone on every job — less a security issue than a performance one, but combined with external pull mirrors can expose full repository history to pipeline scripts.
Job runs always (even on failure) — if this is a deployment or notification job, it may send failure artifacts or partial build outputs to production or external services.
CI_JOB_TOKEN referenced in script context — verify the token is not echoed to logs or passed to untrusted external services. CI_JOB_TOKEN grants access to the project's API and registry.

docker_compose.description: Docker Compose service running in privileged mode — same as running a container with --privileged, granting full host kernel access and enabling container escape.
Container uses host network mode — shares the host's network stack, bypassing Docker network isolation. Container can bind to any host port and access all host network interfaces.
Docker socket mounted into container — allows the container process to control the Docker daemon, create privileged containers, and achieve full host compromise.
Host /etc directory bind-mounted into container — container can read or modify /etc/passwd, /etc/sudoers, /etc/cron.d and other security-critical host configuration.
Host /proc filesystem mounted into container — allows reading kernel configuration, modifying system parameters via /proc/sys, and accessing /proc/sysrq-trigger.
no-new-privileges set to false — container processes can gain additional privileges through setuid binaries or file capabilities. Should be set to true for all production containers.
Container service explicitly configured to run as root (user: root or user: '0') — any process compromise grants root within the container, simplifying escape attempts.
Host root filesystem (/) bind-mounted into container — complete access to the host filesystem for reading and writing, equivalent to physical host access.
Dangerous Linux capability added via cap_add. CAP_SYS_ADMIN is near-equivalent to privileged; CAP_NET_ADMIN allows network stack manipulation; CAP_SYS_PTRACE enables inter-process memory inspection.
security_opt present but check if seccomp and apparmor profiles are also applied. Missing seccomp profile allows the default unrestricted syscall set, increasing kernel attack surface.
Hardcoded password in Docker Compose environment variable — visible in docker inspect output, container environment, and process listing. Use Docker secrets or external secrets manager.
Hardcoded secret or API key in Docker Compose service environment — stored in plaintext in the compose file and visible to anyone with repository or host access.
